text.title: Spring @Async Patterns
text.summary: Rules for @Async configuration, named executor beans, return types, and proxy-bypass gotchas.

rule.statement: Declare one named ThreadPoolTaskExecutor bean per workload type (e.g., emailExecutor, reportExecutor). Never share the default Spring executor across unrelated tasks.
rule.rationale: Sharing the default executor causes head-of-line blocking: slow report tasks block fast email tasks, degrading latency for high-priority work.

rule.statement: Configure each executor with corePoolSize, maxPoolSize, queueCapacity, and threadNamePrefix. Set CallerRunsPolicy as the rejection handler to apply back-pressure instead of dropping tasks.
rule.rationale: Without rejection policy, task overflow silently drops work. CallerRunsPolicy slows the caller, propagating back-pressure.

rule.statement: @Async methods that produce a result must return CompletableFuture<T>. @Async void methods must catch all exceptions internally — they swallow exceptions silently otherwise.
rule.rationale: CompletableFuture propagates exceptions to the caller. void @Async methods have no mechanism to surface exceptions after they're thrown.

rule.statement: Never call an @Async method from within the same bean (self-invocation). The Spring AOP proxy is bypassed and the call runs synchronously on the caller's thread.
rule.rationale: Spring @Async works via a proxy that wraps the bean. Calling the method directly on this (not the proxy) bypasses the proxy entirely.

rule.statement: @Async methods must be public and non-final. Marking them private or final prevents proxy interception.
rule.rationale: Spring AOP creates a subclass proxy; overriding private or final methods is not possible in Java.

rule.statement: In tests, replace the async executor with SyncTaskExecutor or use CompletableFuture.get() with a timeout to make async behaviour synchronous and deterministic.
rule.rationale: Async tests that rely on timing are flaky. SyncTaskExecutor runs the task on the test thread, making assertions straightforward.
